rows.v: Radio shorthand meaning 'message received and understood.' One of the most recognizable bits of military and aviation communication.
'Proceed to checkpoint Bravo.' 'Roger,' the pilot replied.
From the older phonetic alphabet, where 'Roger' stood for the letter 'R' — for 'received'. The word stuck even after the alphabet changed to 'Romeo'.
'Copy' means a message was received; 'Wilco' (will comply) means received and the order will be carried out. A subtle but important distinction.
'Copy that,' she said, noting the coordinates. 'Wilco,' he added, already moving to comply.
'Copy' from receiving/copying down a transmission; 'Wilco' is a contraction of 'will comply'. 'Roger Wilco' redundantly means 'understood, and I'll do it'.
'Over' means 'I'm done speaking, your turn'; 'Out' means 'this conversation is finished.' Contrary to movies, 'over and out' is a contradiction.
'Returning to base, over.' ... 'Understood. Out.'
Precise radio procedure: 'over' invites a reply, 'out' ends the exchange. Saying 'over and out' — common in film — is technically nonsensical, mixing the two.
'Affirmative' means yes; 'negative' means no. Used instead of the easily-misheard 'yeah' or 'nah' over crackly radio.
'Do you have visual?' 'Affirmative.' 'Any casualties?' 'Negative.'
From 'affirm' and 'negate'. The full, distinct words resist being garbled or misheard over noisy radio far better than a quick 'yes' or 'no'.
The international distress call signaling a life-threatening emergency, repeated three times. The most serious call a pilot or sailor can make.
'Mayday, mayday, mayday,' the pilot transmitted as both engines failed.
From the French 'm'aidez' / 'venez m'aider' (help me / come help me), chosen in the 1920s because it sounded clear and distinct over radio to English and French ears.
The phonetic alphabet (Alpha, Bravo, Charlie...) spells out letters with distinct words so they can't be confused over radio. Essential for clear comms.
He spelled the callsign 'Foxtrot Golf November' so there could be no mistaking it.
The NATO phonetic alphabet, standardized in the 1950s, assigns an unmistakable word to each letter — vital when a misheard letter could cost lives.
The proper way to ask for a message to be repeated. Pilots and soldiers never say 'repeat' on the radio — that word means something else entirely.
'You're breaking up — say again your last,' she radioed.
'Repeat' is avoided because, in artillery contexts, it specifically means 'fire again'. 'Say again' safely requests a repeat without risking a deadly misunderstanding.
An instruction to set a transponder code, which identifies an aircraft on radar. 'Squawk 7700' signals an emergency to air traffic control.
Air traffic control instructed the pilot to squawk a specific code for identification.
From a WWII transponder system codenamed 'Parrot' — hence the bird-like 'squawk' for the electronic signal an aircraft emits to identify itself on radar.
An aircraft's height above a reference point, usually sea level or the ground. The vertical dimension pilots constantly monitor and manage.
The pilot climbed to a cruising altitude of 35,000 feet.
From Latin 'altitudo' (height), from 'altus' (high). One of the fundamental measurements of flight, distinct from altitude above ground versus above sea level.
The compass direction an aircraft is pointed, measured in degrees from 0 to 360. 'Heading 270' means flying due west.
Air traffic control instructed her to turn to a heading of 090, due east.
From 'head' — the direction the nose 'heads' toward. Expressed in degrees so pilots and controllers share a precise, unambiguous sense of direction.
The speed of an aircraft relative to the air it moves through — which differs from its speed over the ground, especially in strong winds. Critical for safe flight.
A strong headwind meant her airspeed was high while her speed over the ground crawled.
A compound of 'air' + 'speed'. The distinction from ground speed matters enormously — a wing's lift depends on airspeed, not how fast the ground passes below.
An aircraft's orientation relative to the horizon — its pitch (nose up/down) and roll (banking). Not to be confused with altitude (height).
He corrected the aircraft's attitude, leveling the wings and lowering the nose.
From the same root as 'attitude' (a posture or disposition) — here, the plane's physical posture in the air. A classic source of confusion with the similar-sounding 'altitude'.
A dangerous loss of lift when a wing exceeds its critical angle and airflow separates — not an engine failure, but an aerodynamic one. The plane stops flying.
Pulling the nose up too steeply, the trainee induced a stall and the aircraft began to drop.
From 'stall' (to stop or stick). Crucially, an aerodynamic stall is about the wing losing lift, not the engine quitting — a frequent public misconception.
Instrument Landing System — radio guidance that helps pilots land precisely in poor visibility, providing both horizontal and vertical alignment to the runway.
In thick fog, she flew the ILS approach, following the beams down to the runway.
An acronym for the 'instrument landing system'. By following its radio beams, pilots can align with and descend to a runway they may not even be able to see.
Flight Level — a standardized altitude expressed in hundreds of feet at a common pressure setting, used at higher altitudes. 'Flight Level 350' is roughly 35,000 feet.
The jet leveled off at Flight Level 390 for the long cruise.
Above a transition altitude, planes use 'flight levels' on a standard pressure setting, so all aircraft share the same reference and stay safely separated vertically.
Aborting a landing attempt to climb back up and try again, when the approach isn't safe. A normal, trained-for maneuver, not a failure.
With another aircraft still on the runway, the pilot executed a go-around and circled back.
Descriptive: the aircraft 'goes around' for another attempt. A routine safety maneuver — far better to abort and retry than force an unsafe landing.
Reconnaissance — gathering information about an area, enemy, or situation, often before an operation. Scouting to reduce the unknowns.
The team ran recon on the compound for two days before the mission.
A clipping of 'reconnaissance', from French 'reconnaître' (to recognize, explore). The vital practice of scouting ahead to learn before acting.
Situation Report — a concise update on the current state of affairs: position, status, threats, and needs. Keeps everyone informed.
'Give me a sitrep,' the commander ordered, wanting the latest on the ground.
A compound abbreviation of 'situation report'. One of many '-rep' military shorthands, it delivers a fast, structured snapshot of current conditions.
ROE — the directives defining when, where, and how force may be used. They govern what soldiers are and aren't permitted to do in a given operation.
Strict rules of engagement meant they couldn't fire unless fired upon first.
Descriptive: the 'rules' governing 'engagement' (combat). ROE translate law, policy, and strategy into concrete limits on the use of force.
'Cover' protects from enemy fire (a solid wall); 'concealment' only hides you from view (a bush). Confusing the two can be fatal.
He ducked behind the concrete barrier for cover, not just the flimsy bushes that offered mere concealment.
A critical distinction: 'cover' physically stops bullets, while 'concealment' only blocks line of sight. A soldier must always know which one they have.
The side of a military formation, or to attack from the side. 'Flanking' an enemy means hitting them from a vulnerable angle rather than head-on.
While one squad held the front, the other moved to flank the enemy from the right.
From the anatomical 'flank' (the side of the body). Attacking the exposed side of an enemy force is one of the oldest and most effective tactics in warfare.
'Exfil' (exfiltration) is the planned withdrawal from an operational area; 'infil' (infiltration) is moving in. Getting out is often the hardest part.
After securing the intel, they moved to the exfil point for extraction.
Clippings of 'exfiltration' and 'infiltration'. The planned routes and methods for slipping into and back out of an objective area, often the riskiest phases.
A warning that friendly forces are dangerously near the target of an air strike or artillery fire, requiring extra caution to avoid hitting one's own troops.
He called in the strike 'danger close,' the enemy nearly on top of his own position.
Descriptive: friendlies are 'dangerously close' to the impact zone. The call signals that supporting fire risks harming the very troops it's meant to protect.
Non-Commissioned Officer — an enlisted leader (like a sergeant) who rose through the ranks, rather than being commissioned as an officer. The 'backbone' of the military.
The young lieutenant leaned heavily on his experienced NCO's hard-won wisdom.
An acronym for 'non-commissioned officer'. NCOs lead from experience rather than commission, and are often called the backbone that holds a unit together.
'CO' is the Commanding Officer (in charge); 'XO' is the Executive Officer (second-in-command, handling day-to-day operations). The top of a unit's leadership.
The CO set the mission's intent while the XO handled the detailed planning.
Acronyms for 'commanding officer' and 'executive officer'. The CO holds ultimate authority; the XO is the deputy who runs the organization's daily machinery.
Zulu Time — Coordinated Universal Time (UTC), used as a single global time reference so forces in different zones can coordinate without confusion.
The operation was set for 0600 Zulu, the same instant everywhere regardless of local time.
'Zulu' is the phonetic letter 'Z', the designator for the UTC time zone. Using one universal clock prevents deadly errors when units span the globe.
Military time uses a 24-hour clock spoken in a distinct way — '1300 hours' for 1 p.m., '0600' as 'zero six hundred.' It eliminates a.m./p.m. confusion.
'We move at 2200 hours,' she said — ten o'clock at night, no ambiguity.
The 24-hour clock spoken military-style. Saying 'oh-six-hundred' removes any chance of confusing morning and evening, critical when timing must be exact.
The hierarchy of authority through which orders pass, from top to bottom. Respecting the chain of command keeps a large organization coordinated.
He took his concern up the chain of command rather than going straight to the general.
Descriptive: the 'chain' of linked authority running through the 'command' structure. Each link reports to the one above, keeping orders and accountability orderly.
After-Action Review — a structured debrief held after an operation to analyze what happened, what worked, what didn't, and how to improve. Learning from every mission.
In the AAR, the team honestly dissected every mistake to do better next time.
An acronym for 'after-action review'. This disciplined, blame-light analysis of an event became so effective it's now widely borrowed by businesses and other fields.
Radio slang for 'On the Move' (the phonetic letters O and M). 'We're Oscar Mike' means a unit is underway.
'Oscar Mike,' he radioed as the convoy rolled out.
From the phonetic alphabet: 'Oscar' (O) and 'Mike' (M) spell 'OM', short for 'on the move'. A crisp way to report that a unit is now underway.
'FUBAR' and 'SNAFU' are colorful acronyms describing things gone badly wrong — 'SNAFU' meaning a situation that's a mess, 'FUBAR' meaning thoroughly broken.
The plan went completely FUBAR the moment the weather turned.
WWII-era acronyms (politely, 'Fouled Up Beyond All Recognition' and 'Situation Normal: All Fouled Up'). Wry soldier humor for the chaos and mishaps of military life.
An all-purpose Army exclamation expressing enthusiasm, agreement, or motivation — meaning roughly 'yes,' 'understood,' or 'hell yeah.' (The Marines say 'Oorah.')
'Are we ready to move?' 'Hooah!' the squad roared back.
Of debated origin, possibly from 'HUA' (Heard, Understood, Acknowledged). This versatile battle cry conveys spirit and assent — the Marine equivalent is 'Oorah'.
Military slang for a kilometer. 'The objective is two klicks north' means two kilometers away. Standard distance shorthand in the field.
'Target's about three klicks east,' the scout reported.
Slang for 'kilometer', of uncertain origin — possibly from the clicking adjustment of a rifle sight or map tools. The standard field term for a kilometer of distance.
'Got your six' means 'I've got your back' — your 'six' is the position directly behind you, like six o'clock on a clock face. A pledge of protection.
'Don't worry, I've got your six,' she said, covering his rear as he advanced.
From the clock-position system pilots use, where 12 is ahead and 6 is directly behind. 'Watch your six' warns of a threat to your rear; 'got your six' promises cover.
'POG' (Person Other than Grunt) is slang for non-combat support troops; a 'grunt' is an infantry soldier. The terms mark a good-natured (and sometimes pointed) rivalry.
The grunts ribbed the POGs about their cushy desks far from the front lines.
'Grunt' evokes the infantryman's hard, grunting labor; 'POG' (person other than grunt) covers everyone else. The banter reflects an age-old combat-vs-support divide.
